title: Racial discrimination and body appreciation: testing direct and, via coping strategies, indirect links in racialised minority adults from the United Kingdom
abstract: Racial discrimination is associated with adverse health outcomes, including negative body image, but less is known about the extent to which racial discrimination is associated with positive body image. The aim of this preregistered study was to test the association between racial discrimination and body appreciation (i.e., a facet of positive body image) and to explore whether different coping strategies mediated any direct relationship. An online sample of racialised minority adults living in the United Kingdom (N = 585) were asked to complete the Everyday Discrimination Scale, Body Appreciation Scale-2, and the Coping Orientation to Problems Experienced inventory. Preliminary linear modelling showed that seeking emotional social support, positive reinterpretation and growth, turning to religion, and focusing on and venting of emotions were significantly associated with body appreciation. However, findings from subsequent path analysis indicated that racial discrimination was not directly associated with body appreciation. In men only, the direct relationship between racial discrimination and body appreciation was mediated by focus on and venting of emotions. These findings suggest that experiences of racial discrimination may not be associated with positive body image outcomes, at least in racialised minority adults from the United Kingdom. We discuss possible reasons for this, as well as the generally null mediating effects.

Racial discrimination and body appreciation: Testing direct, and, via coping 
strategies, indirect links in racialised minority adults from the 
United Kingdom
Nadia Craddock a,*
, Jamie Chan b, Viren Swami c,d
a Centre for Appearance Research, University of the West of England, Bristol, United Kingdom

A R T I C L E  I N F O
Keywords:
Racial discrimination
Racism
Positive body image
Body appreciation
Coping strategies
A B S T R A C T
Racial discrimination is associated with adverse health outcomes, including negative body image, but less is 
known about the extent to which racial discrimination is associated with positive body image. The aim of this 
preregistered study was to test the association between racial discrimination and body appreciation (i.e., a facet 
of positive body image) and to explore whether different coping strategies mediated any direct relationship. An 
online sample of racialised minority adults living in the United Kingdom (N = 576) were asked to complete the 
Everyday Discrimination Scale, Body Appreciation Scale-2, and the Coping Orientation to Problems Experienced 
inventory. Preliminary linear modelling showed that seeking emotional social support, positive reinterpretation 
and growth, turning to religion, and focusing on and venting of emotions were significantly associated with body 
appreciation. However, findings from subsequent path analysis indicated that racial discrimination was not 
directly associated with body appreciation. In men only, the direct relationship between racial discrimination 
and body appreciation was mediated by focus on and venting of emotions. These findings suggest that experi­
ences of racial discrimination may not be associated with positive body image outcomes, at least in racialised 
minority adults from the United Kingdom. We discuss possible reasons for this, as well as the generally null 
mediating effects.
1. Introduction
Racial discrimination refers to inequitable, unfair, and unjust treat­
ment of individuals or groups based on their racialised group1 (Williams 
et al., 2019). Often conceptualised as the behavioural manifestation of 
racism, racial discrimination relies on an ideology of racial superiority – 
oftentimes white supremacy – as well as social structures and interper­
sonal behaviours associated with dominance, oppression, and prejudice 
(Kirkinis et al., 2021; Pieterse & Powell, 2016; Reskin, 2012). Accord­
ingly, racial discrimination occurs at multiple levels (i.e., individu­
al/interpersonal, institutional, and cultural; Williams et al., 2019), 
across multiple domains (e.g., education, employment, housing, crim­
inal justice; Reskin, 2012), and in different forms (e.g., subtle experi­
ences of mistreatment, explicit forms of derogation and intimidation, 
rejection or isolation, structural barriers in the forms of lack of oppor­
tunities or hostile environments; Carter et al., 2019; Essed, 1991).
There is now a wealth of evidence showing that racial discrimination 
contributes to health disparities across a range of physical and mental 
health outcomes (for reviews and meta-analyses, see Carter et al., 2019; 
Cave et al., 2020; de Freitas et al., 2018; Kirkinis et al., 2021; Paradies 
et al., 2015). For instance, one recent meta-analysis found robust direct 
relationships between racial discrimination and poorer mental health 
(r = .207) and poorer physical health (r = .067) outcomes (Carter et al., 
2019). Much of this work theorises racial discrimination as a chronic 
social stressor for its targets, who perceive that their individual or col­
lective resources are insufficient to meet the demands of their environ­
ment, thereby threatening well-being (Clark et al., 1999; Harrell, 2000). 
That is, racial discrimination is believed to create a stress reaction in

which the targets of discrimination struggle to cope with the physical 
and emotional strain of racism (Carter et al., 2019), which in turn has 
downstream effects on health. Experiences of racial discrimination may 
also leave individuals with fewer opportunities or resources for making 
healthy behaviour choices (Pascoe & Richman, 2009).
1.1. Racial discrimination and body image
Consistent with Landor and colleagues’ (2024) Sociostructural- 
Intersectional Body Image (SIBI) framework, factors such as racism 
are vital to consider for a more comprehensive understanding of body 
image in racialised minority populations. Indeed, a small body of work 
has documented an association between greater experiences of racial 
discrimination and negative body image (e.g., body dissatisfaction, body 
shame; for a meta-analysis, see Mason et al., 2021). Much of this work is 
situated in the United States (U.S.) and studies tend to centre a specific 
racialised minority group and focus on a single gender. For example, 
Velez and colleagues (2015) reported that more frequent experiences of 
racial discrimination were associated with greater body shame in Latina 
women in the U.S. Similarly, Cheng and colleagues found a positive 
association between experiencing racial discrimination and body 
dissatisfaction (Cheng et al., 2014), and between experiencing racial 
discrimination and body shame (Cheng et al., 2017) in two samples of 
Asian American women. Additionally, in a sample of Asian American 
men, Cheng et al. (2016) found a positive association between racial 
discrimination and drive for muscularity. Bhambhani and colleagues 
(2019) also found that experiencing racial discrimination in sexual and 
dating contexts was related to higher body dissatisfaction in a sample of 
U.S. gay/bisexual men of colour.
In explanation of these associations, scholars have generally drawn 
on the notion that racial discrimination acts as a (minority) stressor that 
leads to negative outcomes. For instance, Mason and colleagues (2018)
proposed a model that links experiences of discrimination with body 
image concerns via two separate pathways. First, experiences of 
discrimination heighten the internalisation of unhealthy appearance 
ideals, possibly as a means of avoiding future discrimination (Mason & 
Lewis, 2016). Second, experiences of discrimination precipitate mal­
adaptive affect regulation strategies that, in turn, increase states of 
negative affect that heighten body surveillance and body image con­
cerns (Mason et al., 2019). Although this model was originally devel­
oped to explain the incidence of disordered eating in sexual minority 
women (Mason et al., 2018), evidence of an association between racial 
discrimination and body dissatisfaction has been interpreted as consis­
tent with this hypothesised model (Mason et al., 2021).
To date, however, less attention has been paid to the possibility that 
racial discrimination may also affect positive aspects of body image. 
Positive body image is a multifaceted construct that can be broadly 
defined as an “overarching love and respect for the body” (Tylka, 2018, 
p. 9) that includes appreciation of the body and its functions, acceptance 
of the body despite its imperfections, and protective behaviours such as 
intuitive eating. Although often inversely correlated with negative body 
image, positive body image has been conceptualised as a distinct 
construct that exists on a separate continuum (Tylka & Wood-Barcalow, 
2015a). As such, it is possible to simultaneously experience aspects of 
both negative body image (e.g., weight and shape concerns) and positive 
body image (e.g., functionality appreciation). Importantly, positive 
body image is associated with a range of beneficial psychological and 
behavioural health outcomes including greater life satisfaction, higher 
self-esteem, self-compassion and emotional wellbeing, and a more 
robust ability to filter out harmful appearance pressures (Davis et al., 
2020; Linardon et al., 2022; Modica & Markley, 2024).
Examining the extent to which racial discrimination may (or may not 
be) associated with positive body image would help broaden the focus of 
extant research beyond experiences of psychopathology. Furthermore, it 
may help to more fully understand the ways in which racism affects the 
ways racialised minority individuals inhabit and relate to their bodies 
(Landor et al., 2024; Ramseyer Winter et al., 2019). Based on available 
theorising (Mason et al., 2018), we should expect that greater racial 
discrimination should be associated with lower indices of positive body 
image because such experiences weaken one’s ability or willingness to 
reject unhealthy (racialised) appearance ideals. Experiences of 
discrimination may also damage the skills and tools that individuals use 
to build up resilience in terms of body image experiences or because they 
limit opportunities to overturn racialised embodiment (Chan et al., 
2023).
The limited available evidence supports the idea that experiences of 
racial discrimination will be associated with lower indices of positive 
body image. Among young African American women, for example, Dunn 
and colleagues (2019) reported that experiences of gendered racial 
microaggressions (i.e., everyday verbal, behavioural, and environ­
mental expressions of systematic oppression based on racialised status 
and gender) were significantly associated with lower appearance 
contingent self-worth and lower body appreciation. However, this area 
of research remains in its infancy and there is a need to more fully assess 
the extent to which racial discrimination is reliably associated with 
positive body image outcomes. The first aim of the current study, 
therefore, was to examine whether racial discrimination is associated 
with positive body image in a diverse sample of racialised minority 
adults.
1.2. Coping with racial discrimination
A secondary objective of the present study was to examine whether 
coping strategies mediate any putative link between racial discrimina­
tion and positive body image. This is important because understanding 
how individuals respond to chronic stressful situations may help 
scholars and practitioners to identify support strategies that could help 
alleviate negative health outcomes and promote well-being. In broad 
outline, the Transactional Model of Stress and Coping (Lazarus & Folk­
man, 1984) posits that individuals respond to stressful events by 
enacting coping strategies, which can be defined as “cognitive and 
behavioural efforts to master, reduce, or tolerate the internal and/or 
external demands that are created by the stressful transaction” 
(Folkman, 1984, p. 843). In terms of body image outcomes specifically, a 
focus on coping strategies is warranted given that existing models sug­
gest that (racial) discrimination heighten the use of maladaptive 
emotion regulation as a coping strategy, which in turn may lead to body 
image concerns (Mason et al., 2018; 2021).
Coping strategies and behaviours are categorised in many different 
ways in the scientific literature, but there is a broad interest in whether 
(and in what contexts) coping strategies are helpful or harmful in 
response to different stressors (Skinner et al., 2003). Lazarus and Folk­
man (1984) distinguished between problem-focused strategies (i.e., 
eliminating the stressor or reducing its impact) and emotion-focused 
strategies (i.e., preventing, minimising, or reducing the emotional 
anguish caused by a stressor). Carver (1997) later added a third broad 
category of dysfunctional coping strategies, which alludes to maladap­
tive responses to a stressor. These contrasting coping strategies are 
typically conceptualised as an approach-avoidance dichotomy (Moos & 
Holahan, 2003). The available research suggests that racialised minority 
individuals reactively respond to racism and discrimination using a 
variety of coping strategies (e.g., Jacob et al., 2023), although they may 
be more likely to utilise emotion-focused, avoidant strategies (e.g., 
avoiding thinking about or engaging in the problem and feeling sorry for 
oneself) to divert attention away from stressful experiences (Vassilli`ere 
et al., 2016). Avoidance-based coping, in turn, is associated with 
increased psychological distress (e.g., Thomas et al., 2008).
Research suggests that associations between racism and wellbeing 
can be explained by some coping strategies, such as behavioural disen­
gagement and acceptance coping, amongst lesbian, gay, and bisexual 
people of colour (Huynh & Lee, 2023). However, to date, we are not 
aware of any research that has specifically examined whether different

coping strategies mediate a relationship between racial discrimination 
and body image outcomes. Certainly, there is evidence to suggest that 
the use of avoidant, emotion-focused coping strategies is associated with 
more negative body image, whereas the use of problem-focused strate­
gies is not significantly associated with body image outcomes (e.g., Koff 
& Sangani, 1997). Indeed, emotion-focused, avoidant strategies (e.g., 
emotional eating) appear to be a common response to stressors, at least 
in women (Smith-Jackson et al., 2011), although this typically results in 
cycles that contribute to body shame (Bailey et al., 2016). However, how 
this body of evidence might transfer to experiences of racial discrimi­
nation and positive body image outcomes is currently unknown. The 
most likely possibility is that racial discrimination is associated with 
emotion-focused strategies, which in turn are associated with lower 
positive body image.
1.3. The present study
In response to calls for more comprehensive understandings on the 
body image experiences of racialised minority communities (Landor 
et al. 2024), the present preregistered study had two related objectives. 
We first sought to examine the extent to which experiences of racial 
discrimination are associated with positive body image, which we 
operationalised through the facet of body appreciation (Tylka & 
Wood-Barcalow, 2015b). Next, we tested whether coping strategies 
mediate the putative link between racial discrimination and body 
appreciation. Using an exploratory approach, we examined a range of 
lower-order coping strategies (Carver & Scheier, 1994) to identify those 
significantly associated with body appreciation before constructing a 
hypothesised mediation model linking racial discrimination and body 
appreciation via coping strategies.
In an additional departure from the existing research that has 
focused on the experiences of racialised minority communities in the U. 
S., the context of the present study is the United Kingdom (U.K.). In the 
U.K., racism remains a prevalent facet of life for people from racialised 
minority backgrounds, including in terms of structural effects (e.g., in­
come, employment, education, health, and housing; Finney et al., 2023) 
and interpersonal effects (e.g., 47 % of racialised minority respondents 
reported experiencing racial discrimination; Ellingworth et al., 2023). 
Given this context, it will be useful to examine the extent to which ex­
periences of racial discrimination are both directly and indirectly asso­
ciated with positive body image outcomes. Here, we hypothesised that 
experiences of racial discrimination in racialised minority individuals in 
the U.K. would be associated with lower body appreciation (H1) and 
that coping strategies would mediate the direct relationship (H2). In 
terms of H2, we preliminarily hypothesised that emotion-focused, 
avoidant strategies would emerge as significant mediators.
2. Method
2.1. Participants
A total of 604 respondents provided informed consent and completed 
the survey. However, we removed 19 respondents who failed one or 
more attention checks, four who identified as belonging to a mixed 
racialised group, and five who preferred not to disclose their racialised 
identity and/or gender identity. This resulted in an analytic sample of 
576. Respondents belonging to a mixed racialised group were excluded 
in the present study due to their unique experiences of racialisation and 
racial discrimination (e.g., Campion, 2019) and those who did not 
disclose their gender identity (n = 2) were removed due to their small 
number. Therefore, we retained a dataset consisting of 288 women and 
288 men (Mage = 33.9 years, SD = 9.8), all of whom were of racialised 
minority status in the context of the U.K. Specifically, 6.9 % identified as 
Arab or Middle Eastern, 59.7 % identified as Asian, 33.4 % as Black, and 
0.3 % as another racialised (minoritised) identity. Additional participant 
demographics are reported in Table 1.
2.2. Materials
2.2.1. Body appreciation
To measure a facet of positive body image, participants were asked to 
complete 
the 
Body 
Appreciation 
Scale-2 
(BAS-2; 
Tylka 
& 
Wood-Barcalow, 2015b). This instrument has 10 items that assess 
acceptance of one’s body, respect and care for one’s body, and protec­
tion of one’s body from unrealistic appearance ideals (sample item: “I 
respect my body”). All items were rated on a 5-point scale, ranging from 
1 (never) to 5 (always). Scores on the BAS-2 has been shown to have a 
unidimensional factor structure in diverse populations, including in the 
U.K. (Swami et al., 2023). There is also evidence of strong construct 
validity (Tylka & Wood-Barcalow, 2015b). An overall score was 
computed as the mean of all items with higher scores reflecting greater 
body appreciation. McDonald’s ω for BAS-2 scores in the present study 
was .97 (95 % CI [.96, .97]) in women and .96 (95 % CI [.96, .97]) in 
men.
2.2.2. Racial discrimination
Experiences of racial discrimination were assessed with the Everyday 
Discrimination Scale (EDS; Williams et al., 1997), one of the most 
frequently used instruments for measuring perceived discrimination 
(Bastos et al., 2010). This is a 9-item self-report scale that measures 
perceptions of day-to-day discrimination and can be used as a general or

focused measure of discrimination. Adopting the latter approach, par­
ticipants in this study were asked, “In your day-to-day life, how often 
have any of the following things happened to you because of your 
race/ethnicity?” (e.g., being treated with less courtesy), with six 
response options ranging from never (1) to almost every day (6). Scores 
on the EDS have been shown to have a unidimensional factor structure 
and adequate evidence of construct validity (e.g., Gonzales et al., 2016; 
Krieger et al., 2005), including in racialised minority communities in the 
U.K. (Jaspal et al., 2021). An overall score was computed as the mean of 
all items, with higher scores indicating more frequent experiences of 
everyday racial discrimination. McDonald’s ω for the EDS was .93 (95 % 
CI [.92, .94]) in women and .92 (95 % CI [.90, .93]) in men.
2.2.3. Coping strategies
Coping strategies were measured using the 60-item Coping Orien­
tation to Problems Experienced (COPE) inventory (Carver et al., 1989), a 
theory-driven measure of various coping strategies used to manage 
difficult situations. The instrument consists of 15 subscales, described in 
Table 2. Each subscale included four items, with responses ranging from 
1 (I usually don’t do this at all) to 4 (I usually do this a lot). Subscale 
scores were computed as the mean of relevant items. Higher scores on 
each subscale reflect a greater likelihood of using that type of coping 
strategy in response to stressful situations. Although there is some 
debate around the appropriate lower- and higher-order factor structure 
of the COPE (e.g., Kr¨ageloh, 2011), the 15 lower-order subscales have 
generally been found to have adequate composite reliability and pro­
vides broad coverage of adaptive and maladaptive coping strategies 
(Carver et al., 1989). McDonald’s ω for the COPE subscales in the present 
study are reported in Table 2.
2.2.4. Demographics
Participants reported their demographic information consisting of 
gender identity (i.e., woman, man, identify my gender in another way), age, 
racialised identity, highest educational qualification, marital status, 
sexual orientation, and whether they identified as a D/deaf or disabled 
person or whether they had a long-term health condition.
2.3. Procedure
Ethics approval was obtained from the research ethics panel at the 
first author’s institution (Ref: CHSS.23.08.006) and the study was pre­
registered at [redacted]. All data were collected on Prolific, a crowd- 
working platform that allows researchers to recruit participants (Palan 
& Schitter, 2018) between January 3–5, 2024. The project was adver­
tised as a study on “racial discrimination and well-being” and included 
an estimated duration. Potential respondents on Prolific were 
pre-screened to ensure that only participants who identified as members 
of a racialised minority, aged 18 years or over, who were citizens and 
residents of the U.K., and who were fluent in English were able to 
complete the survey. Two attention check items were placed between 
items of the COPE, and one was placed between items of the EDS. Pro­
lific ID codes were examined to ensure that no participant took the 
survey more than once. After providing digital informed consent, par­
ticipants provided their demographic details before completing the 
additional measures, which were presented in a randomised order for 
each participant. The survey was anonymous and, in exchange for 
completion, participants were paid £ 1.50.
2.4. Analytic strategy
We first tested for mean differences between women and men across 
all variables included in the present study and controlled for the false 
discovery rate (FDR) using the Benjamini-Hochberg procedure 
(Benjamini & Hochberg, 1995). Next, we computed bivariate correla­
tions between racial discrimination, the 15 coping strategies, and body 
appreciation for men and women separately. Additionally, we assessed 
associations between the coping strategies and body appreciation using 
linear model analyses, conducted using the robust package (Wang et al., 
2013) in R (R Development Core Team, 2021), which deals with 
non-normality. To identify which of the different coping strategies were 
associated with body appreciation, we constructed a linear model where 
the 15 coping strategies were modelled simultaneously as predictor 
variables and body appreciation was modelled as the criterion variable. 
Only coping strategies that were significantly associated with body 
appreciation were retained as mediators in subsequent analyses.

coping strategies (see Fig. 1). To test the fit of this model to the data, we 
used path analysis conducted using the lavaan package (Rosseel, 2012) 
in R. As there were no missing data, Maximum Likelihood with Robust 
standard errors (MLR) was used to account for non-normality in the 
data. We assessed the fit of the model using the model chi-square value, 
the comparative fit index (CFI; with values close to or ≥.95 indicating 
good fit), the Stieger-Lind root mean square error of approximation 
(RMSEA) and its 90 % CI (values close to .06 considered to be indicative 
of good fit and up to .08 indicative of adequate fit) and the standardised 
root mean square residual (SRMR; values < .09 indicative of good fit) 
(Hu & Bentler, 1999; Swami & Barron, 2019). If the model needed 
improving, we aimed to add or release constraints for particular paths 
based on modification indices (with values > 3.84 suggesting potential 
improvements in model fit).
Based on the final path analytic model (with adequate fit), we fitted a 
constrained model and an unconstrained model to assess model invari­
ance across women and men. We then compared the fit of the con­
strained model to that of the unconstrained model using a likelihood 
ratio test (i.e., chi-square values) and the comparative fit index, with 
decreases in CFI > .01 indicating worse model fit (Cheung & Rensvold, 
2002). A significantly worse fitting model would indicate invariance 
between men and women. Reactive Monte Carlo simulations in Mplus v. 
8.11 (Muth´en & Muth´en, 2024) showed that our sample size was 
adequate for detecting the hypothesised direct and mediation effects. 
The analysis was based on a significance level of p < .05, statistical 
power at .95, and an effect size of .10. The sample size in this study 
exceeded the minimum required threshold.
3. Results
3.1. Preliminary analyses
Descriptive statistics and FDR-corrected independent-samples t-tests 
are reported in Table 3. Women had significantly higher scores on the 
COPE subscales of social emotional support-seeking, mental disen­
gagement, and focus on and venting of emotions than men, whereas men 
scored significantly higher in the use of humour and alcohol-drug use 
subscales than women. No significant gender differences were found in 
racial discrimination and body appreciation scores.
Bivariate correlations between racial discrimination, the 15 coping 
strategies, and body appreciation are reported in Table 4. For both 
women and men, racial discrimination was not significantly associated 
with body appreciation. In men, racial discrimination was significantly 
associated with higher scores on all coping strategies except active 
coping, planning, positive reinterpretation and growth, and acceptance. 
Men’s body appreciation was also significantly linked with all coping 
strategies except denial, mental disengagement, and focus on and 
venting of emotions. For the coping strategies that were significantly 
correlated with men’s body appreciation, higher scores were linked with 
greater body appreciation, except for behavioural disengagement and 
alcohol-drug use – these were linked with lower body appreciation. For 
women, racial discrimination was significantly associated with greater 
restraint coping, denial, use of humour and alcohol-drug use. Women’s 
body appreciation was significantly associated with all coping strate­
gies, except denial and mental disengagement. For the coping strategies 
that were significantly correlated with women’s body appreciation, 
higher scores were linked with greater body appreciation, except 
behavioural disengagement, focus on and venting of emotions, and 
alcohol-drug use – these strategies were linked with lower body 
appreciation.
The associations between the different coping strategies and body 
appreciation were further tested in a linear model (see Table 5). Notably, 
only the seeking emotional social support (β = 3.01, p = .003), positive 
reinterpretation and growth (β = 3.44, p < .001), turning to religion (β 
= 3.69, p < .001), and focus on and venting of emotions (β = −4.15, p 
< .001) subscales were significantly associated with body appreciation. 
These were modelled as mediators in subsequent analyses.

and the model was invariant between genders (Δχ2 = 77.23, p < .001;

4. Discussion
This preregistered study tested whether racial discrimination was 
significantly associated with body appreciation among a sample of 
racialised minority participants in the U.K. (H1), and whether coping 
strategies mediated this relationship (H2). Contrary to H1, racial 
discrimination was not significantly and directly associated with body 
appreciation. Further, our results only marginally supported H2. While 
preliminary linear modelling showed that several coping strategies were 
significantly associated with body appreciation, just one strategy – focus 
on and venting of emotions – mediated a relationship between racial 
discrimination and body appreciation. Notably, however, multigroup 
path analysis indicated that this result was only significant in men. 
Overall, these findings suggest that racial discrimination is neither 
directly nor, with one minor exception, indirectly associated with body 
appreciation via common coping strategies.
4.1. Reflections on our H1 findings
Our findings showed that racial discrimination was not significantly 
associated with a central facet of positive body image, namely body 
appreciation. This finding is inconsistent with prior theoretical model­
ling suggesting that experiences of racial discrimination should be 
associated with body image outcomes (Mason et al., 2018; 2021). It also 
stands in contrast to the limited research that has centred facets of 
positive body image; specifically, it contrasts with the findings of Dunn 
and colleagues (2019), who showed that gendered racial micro­

ciation in a sample of African American women.
One explanation for our null association between racial discrimina­
tion and body appreciation is that reported experiences of racial 
discrimination were relatively infrequent in our sample. Mean responses 
were just over 2.0 (on a 6-point scale), suggesting that our sample did 
not appear to be chronically affected by racial discrimination. Thus, any 
putative relationship between racial discrimination and body appreci­
ation may have been affected by floor effects. Relatedly, a meta-analytic 
review (Schmitt et al., 2014) found that associations between perceived 
discrimination (including racial discrimination) and positive outcomes 
(e.g., self-esteem, life satisfaction, positive affect) were generally weaker 
than for negative outcomes (e.g., depression, anxiety, psychological 
distress). Therefore, it is possible that examining a relationship between 
racial discrimination and body appreciation (a positive outcome) may 
be more vulnerable to floor effects than a relationship between racial 
discrimination and facets of negative body image, such as body 
dissatisfaction.
Another possible explanation pertains to our chosen measurement of 
racial discrimination. The EDS is a brief, 9-item measure that measures 
the frequency of perceived experiences of mistreatment and negative 
stereotyping. We selected this measure based on its widespread use, 
which allows for comparison across studies, as well as its brevity to 
minimise participant burden. However, the EDS does not capture the 
severity or intensity of racial discrimination, or the associated stress felt 
by individuals. A more comprehensive measure – such as the Schedule of 
Racist Events (Landrine & Klonoff, 1996), which captures the frequency 
as well as the extent to which experiences of racial discrimination are 
felt as stressful – may have garnered different results. Additionally, the 
EDS does not include the impact of anticipated racism, which could 
include racism at the structural or systemic level, as well as interper­
sonal racism. Capturing the stress felt by individuals due to racial 
discrimination and/or anticipated racism, which itself may serve as a 
more chronic stressor, may be more closely related to how individuals 
appreciate, respect, and feel comfortable in their bodies.
Finally, taking our results at face value, it is possible that racial 
discrimination – irrespective of frequency, severity, or associated stress 
– is not associated with body appreciation. Our hypothesising in the 
present study was largely driven by results obtained with indices of 
negative body image, but negative and positive body image do not lay 
on the same continuum (Tylka & Wood-Barcalow, 2015a). Therefore, it 
is possible for a construct to be related to negative body image but not to 
positive body image – as may be the case for racial discrimination – and 
vice versa. Individuals may feel greater body dissatisfaction (i.e., are less 
content with their appearance) in response to racial discrimination 
because such experiences are often prompted by appearance-related 
cues, such as skin colour and racialised facial features. But, by 
contrast, racial discrimination may not as readily affect body appreci­
ation, which is more holistic in nature and reflects a deeper connection 
to one’s body (Tylka & Wood-Barcalow, 2015a).
4.2. Reflections on our H2 findings
Turning to the mediating role of coping, in general, we found that 
coping strategies did not mediate the link between racial discrimination 
and body appreciation, either at subscale or latent factor levels. The 
most likely reason for this is because there was no direct relationship 
between racial discrimination and body appreciation. However, we did 
find that, in men only, focusing on and venting of emotions mediated the 
relationship between racial discrimination and body appreciation.
In our preliminary linear modelling, some coping strategies were 
significantly associated with body appreciation. Specifically, we found 
that seeking emotional social support, positive reinterpretation and 
growth, turning to religion, and focus on and venting of emotions were 
significantly associated with body appreciation. Body appreciation has 
previously been positively associated with proactive coping (Avalos 
et al., 2005) and engagement with religion and/or spirituality 
(Tiggemann & Hage, 2019) in samples of North American and Austra­
lian women, respectively. Therefore, our results support and extend 
these earlier findings to men, as well as a new national context. Further, 
qualitative research has pointed to emotional social support as a factor 
that helps to foster a positive body image (Wood-Barcalow et al., 2010). 
The negative association between focusing on and venting emotions and 
body appreciation is more novel, though it is consistent with research on 
psychological well-being more broadly. Focusing on and venting emo­
tions can be defined as monitoring and expressing distressing emotions. 
While it could be cathartic to release negative emotions, it may lead an 
individual to dwell on negative experiences. Thus, focusing on and 
venting emotions can be considered a less proactive form of coping and

7 
can exacerbate the adverse effects of racial discrimination on an in­
dividual’s well-being (Brondolo et al., 2005; Kaholokula et al., 2017).
Beyond these direct associations between coping strategies and body 
appreciation, however, our results generally showed that coping stra­
tegies did not mediate any relationship between racial discrimination 
and body appreciation – with one exception. Specifically, in men only, 
focusing on and venting of emotions mediated the relationship between 
racial discrimination and body appreciation. Findings from a previous 
meta-analysis suggested that gender differences in coping were influ­
enced by differences in the types of stressful situations (Tamres et al., 
2002); in particular, men were more likely than women to use venting as 
a coping strategy when dealing with social stressors. Interpersonal racial 
discrimination is often positioned as a form of chronic social stress 
(Clark et al., 1999). Therefore, it is plausible that this shaped men’s 
increased focus on and venting of emotions as a coping strategy, which 
in turn was associated with body appreciation outcomes.
It is important to note that the COPE strategies were not tailored to 
focus on coping strategies in relation to racial discrimination; rather, 
items pertain to coping in relation to any stressful situation. Conversely, 
resistance and empowerment against racism can be positioned as a form 
of coping in response to racial discrimination specifically (Suyemoto 
et al., 2022). In a sample of racialised minority women and men from the 
U.K., Chan and colleagues (2023) reported that facets of resistance and 
empowerment against racism (i.e., leadership for resistance against 
racism and interpersonal confrontation of racism) were significantly 
associated with greater body appreciation and body acceptance by 
others.
4.3. Limitations and future directions
There are several important limitations that warrant discussion, 
which may be addressed in subsequent studies. First, as this study was 
cross-sectional in design, the direction of association between variables 
should be understood with caution. Although we developed a hypoth­
esised model that conforms to current theorising, any causal inference 
should be treated with caution. Additionally, studies show that cumu­
lative exposure to racial discrimination has incremental negative long- 
term effects on the mental health of racialised minority people in the 
U.K. (Wallace et al., 2016). Therefore, our findings are limited by 
considering associations between racial discrimination and positive 
body image at a single point in time. As our cross-sectional findings 
present mostly null results, we suggest researchers consider examining 
longitudinal relationships between racial discrimination and multiple 
aspects of both positive and negative body image.
Second, our sample is not representative, meaning that generalisa­
tions cannot be readily made. Further, it was not possible to examine 
potential differences by racialised group due to insufficient sample size 
in each subgroup. Such analyses may have yielded more nuanced re­
sults. For example, data from a large-scale, U.K. based study showed that 
both cross-sectional and longitudinal associations between racial 
discrimination and psychological and physical health outcomes are not 
consistent across racialised groups (Hackett et al., 2020). Similarly, in 
their systematic review based on predominantly U.S. data, Paradies and 
colleagues (2015)
highlighted that racialised group significantly 
moderated associations between racial discrimination and various 
health outcomes. Therefore, it is possible that the association between 
racial discrimination and body appreciation may vary depending on 
racialised identity.
Third, we did not assess skin shade and so we also did not include 
skin shade as a covariate in our analysis. Prior research indicates that 
Black, Asian and other racialised minority adults in the U.K. with darker 
skin report greater exposure to racial discrimination than their racialised 
group peers with light skin (Craddock, Phoenix et al., 2023). Studies also 
show that skin shade is often relevant to body image (e.g., body 
dissatisfaction and body esteem) for racialised minorities in the U.K. 
(Chan & Hurst, 2022; Craddock Gentili et al., 2023). Therefore, 
including skin shade would have been a potentially valuable addition 
and could be explored in future work on racial discrimination and 
positive body image.
4.4. Conclusion
In conclusion, our findings suggest that experiences of racial 
discrimination may not be associated with body appreciation in racial­
ised minority participants from the U.K., and that coping strategies do 
not effectively mediate any direct relationship. In view of this finding, 
one useful avenue for future research would be to replicate the present 
work but include indices of both negative and positive body image, 
which would provide an opportunity to test the extent to which our 
findings are anomalous. In a similar vein, it may be useful to consider 
other facets of positive body image that may be more directly impacted 
by racial discrimination, such as body pride (e.g., see Le et al., 2022). 
More generally, it may also be important to replicate the present finding 
in other national contexts, to help determine whether our findings are 
specific to the U.K. or more broadly generalisable. Certainly, there is 
scope to more fully consider the extent to which racial discrimination 
may – or may not be – associated with indices of positive body image in 
diverse communities.
